compositions.name: Inngest AgentKit
compositions.intent: TypeScript framework for composing multi-agent networks where a Router decides which Agent runs next over a shared State, running on Inngest's durable-execution engine for fault-tolerance and human-in-the-loop pauses.
compositions.description: Inngest AgentKit is an open-source TypeScript framework built on top of the Inngest workflow engine. The core primitives are Agent (an LLM call with prompts, tools, and MCP), Tool, Network (a system of agents sharing State), and Router (code-based, LLM-based, or hybrid logic that picks the next Agent each loop). The execution model is a while-loop over State; when AgentKit functions run inside Inngest, each step becomes a durable, replayable unit and `step.waitForEvent` lets agents pause for hours waiting on a human reply.
primary_use_cases: multi-agent networks with deterministic or hybrid routing
tool-using agents over MCP server registries
long-running agent workflows with human-in-the-loop approval
fault-tolerant production agents on Inngest durable execution
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Network-as-while-loop. Each iteration: Router reads State and picks an Agent (or returns undefined to stop); the chosen Agent makes an inference call with its tools and any MCP servers; tool results and messages are appended to shared State; the loop continues until the Router stops or `maxIter` is hit. When deployed on Inngest, every Agent and tool call is wrapped in `step.*` so the run survives crashes, restarts, and arbitrary `waitForEvent` pauses.

key_concepts.name: Agent
key_concepts.summary: Stateless wrapper around a model with a goal, prompts, and tools (including MCP).

key_concepts.name: Network
key_concepts.summary: While-loop with shared State that calls Agents and Tools until the Router stops.

key_concepts.name: Router
key_concepts.summary: Code-based, LLM-based (default routing agent / ReAct), or hybrid; decides the next Agent.

key_concepts.name: State
key_concepts.summary: Shared kv store + message history; typed; used by Router for routing decisions.

key_concepts.name: MCP-as-tools
key_concepts.summary: Any MCP server (Streamable HTTP / SSE / WS) plugs in as a tool source.

key_concepts.name: waitForEvent / human-in-the-loop
key_concepts.summary: Inngest step that pauses an Agent's tool for hours waiting for an event.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[User input] --> net[Network]
  net --> router{Router}
  router -->|code-based| pick1[Deterministic choice]
  router -->|LLM / ReAct| pick2[Default routing agent]
  router -->|hybrid| pick3[Mix]
  pick1 --> agent[Agent inference]
  pick2 --> agent
  pick3 --> agent
  agent --> tools[Tools / MCP servers]
  tools --> state[Append to shared State]
  state --> hil{Tool needs human?}
  hil -->|yes| wait[step.waitForEvent]
  wait --> state
  hil -->|no| router
  router -->|stop / maxIter| done[Network result]
  agent -.runs as.-> inngest[Inngest durable step]
  inngest -.fault-tolerant.-> done

members.note: When deployed on Inngest, AgentKit agents become fault-tolerant durable workflows that survive restarts; `step.waitForEvent` can pause for hours.

evidence.title: AgentKit README - durable execution
evidence.quote: Combined with the Inngest Dev Server to start locally and its orchestration engine, making your Agents fault-tolerant when deployed.

evidence.title: AgentKit human-in-the-loop - long wait
evidence.quote: The `ask_developer` tool will wait up to 4 hours for a `developer.response` event to be received

members.note: Human-in-the-loop tools built on Inngest `waitForEvent` pause the network until a matching event arrives.

evidence.title: AgentKit HITL - waitForEvent
evidence.quote: Human in the Loop tools are implemented using Inngest's `waitForEvent()` step method

evidence.title: AgentKit HITL - rationale
evidence.quote: Agents such as Support Agents, Coding or Research Agents might require human oversight.

members.note: Agents react to and wait on Inngest events; the Inngest engine triggers and resumes runs on event arrival.

evidence.title: AgentKit HITL - event match
evidence.quote: Wait for developer response event

evidence.title: AgentKit README - waitForEvent for humans
evidence.quote: Inngest's `waitForEvent()` enables the Agent to ask a human for help.

members.note: Scheduling is inherited from Inngest (cron functions with timezones and jitter), not surfaced as a first-class AgentKit primitive in the AgentKit docs themselves; honest downgrade from first-class.

evidence.title: Inngest scheduled functions — native cron
evidence.quote: You can create scheduled jobs using cron schedules within Inngest natively.

evidence.title: Inngest scheduled functions — timezones
evidence.quote: Inngest's cron schedules also support timezones, allowing you to schedule work in whatever timezone you need work to run in.

members.note: Networks support a `maxIter` option to cap loop iterations and avoid infinite loops; networks are while-loops that the router exits when work is done.

evidence.title: AgentKit networks - maxIter
evidence.quote: Specifying a `maxIter` option is useful when using a [Default Routing Agent] or a [Hybrid Router] to avoid infinite loops.

evidence.title: AgentKit networks - while-loop with memory
evidence.quote: Networks can be thought of as while loops with memory (State) that call Agents and Tools until the Router determines that there is no more work to be done.

evidence.title: AgentKit networks - maxIter limits iterations
evidence.quote: A Network can specify an optional `maxIter` setting to limit the number of iterations.

members.note: Router is the supervisor primitive: code, LLM-based (default routing agent), or hybrid.

evidence.title: AgentKit networks - router role
evidence.quote: A [Router], which chooses whether to stop or select the next agent to run in the loop

evidence.title: AgentKit README - router
evidence.quote: where the autonomy lives, from code-based to LLM-based (ex: ReAct) orchestration

members.note: Tools are built via function-calling; AgentKit also exposes any MCP server as a tool source.

evidence.title: AgentKit tools - purpose
evidence.quote: Tools are functions that extend the capabilities of an Agent. Tools have two core uses: calling code, enabling models to interact with systems like your own database or external APIs.

evidence.title: AgentKit MCP - any MCP server as tools
evidence.quote: Using MCP as tools allows you to use any MCP server as a tool in your AgentKit network, enabling your Agent to access thousands of pre-built tools.

members.note: First-class MCP client; Streamable HTTP / SSE / WS transports; Smithery registry integration.

evidence.title: AgentKit MCP - Claude MCP support
evidence.quote: AgentKit supports using Claude's Model Context Protocol as tools.

evidence.title: AgentKit MCP - transports
evidence.quote: AgentKit supports configuring MCP servers via Streamable HTTP, SSE or WS transports.

members.note: A slow tool/inference step is offloaded so the function pauses rather than holds a blocking connection, and resumes with the result when it lands; each step is an atomic, retriable unit that survives across long-running durable runs.

evidence.title: inngest-agentkit
evidence.quote: step.ai.infer() offloads the inference request to Inngest's infrastructure, pausing your function execution until the request finishes. ... Once the request finishes, your function restarts with the inference result's data.

members.note: Running function (and AgentKit agent) executions can be stopped mid-flight out of band via the REST API, the Dashboard, or by sending a cancellation event, without a redeploy.

evidence.title: Cancellation - Inngest Documentation
evidence.quote: Inngest enables you to cancel running Functions via the API, Dashboard, or based on events:

alternatives.note: Durable-execution peer; Temporal is a workflow engine for multiple languages, AgentKit is TS-only and agent-shaped.
Peer durable-agent platform; Restack runs on Temporal/Kubernetes, AgentKit runs on Inngest.

references.title: inngest/agent-kit
references.quote: Build multi-agent networks with deterministic routing and rich tooling via MCP.

references.title: Introducing AgentKit and step.ai: orchestrating AI with confidence
references.quote: Introducing AgentKit and step.ai: orchestrating AI with confidence

compositions.name: Modal
compositions.intent: Serverless compute platform that turns Python functions into autoscaled cloud containers and exposes Sandboxes as ephemeral, secure runtimes for executing AI-generated code on demand.
compositions.description: Modal is an AI infrastructure platform built by Modal Labs. Developers decorate Python functions with `@app.function` (optionally with a GPU, image, schedule, or `modal.Sandbox` reference) and Modal containerises and runs them in the cloud with sub-second cold starts and autoscaling. For agentic workloads the headline primitive is the Sandbox: a constructor (`Sandbox.create`) that programmatically spins up isolated containers to run untrusted or LLM-generated code, with filesystem, directory, and memory snapshots to checkpoint and restore state.
primary_use_cases: secure sandboxed execution of LLM-generated code
GPU-backed inference and fine-tuning
scheduled / cron-driven serverless jobs
containerised batch compute for agents
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Modal is not itself an agent runtime; it is the substrate. Agent frameworks call `Sandbox.create()` to obtain an ephemeral container, send code or shell commands into it, observe stdout/stderr/files, optionally snapshot the filesystem or memory, and either reuse, detach, or terminate the Sandbox. Scheduled functions use the `schedule=modal.Period(...)` or `modal.Cron(...)` parameter on `@app.function` so the entry-point runs on a cadence without external orchestration.

key_concepts.name: App
key_concepts.summary: Container for a set of related Modal Functions: `app = modal.App("name")`.

key_concepts.name: Function
key_concepts.summary: Python function decorated with `@app.function(...)`; Modal builds an image and runs it in the cloud.

key_concepts.name: Sandbox
key_concepts.summary: Ephemeral isolated container created at runtime via `Sandbox.create` for executing untrusted/agent code.

key_concepts.name: Sandbox snapshots
key_concepts.summary: Filesystem, directory, and memory snapshots that save and restore Sandbox state.

key_concepts.name: Schedule (Period / Cron)
key_concepts.summary: `schedule=modal.Period(...)` or `modal.Cron(...)` on `@app.function` runs the function on a cadence.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[Developer code] --> app[modal.App]
  app --> fn[@app.function]
  fn -->|schedule=Period/Cron| cron[Recurring run]
  fn -->|invoked| run[Containerised execution]
  fn -->|creates| sb[Sandbox.create]
  sb --> iso[Isolated container]
  iso --> exec[Run LLM-generated code / shell]
  exec --> snap{Snapshot?}
  snap -->|filesystem| fs[Filesystem snapshot]
  snap -->|memory| mem[Memory snapshot]
  snap -->|directory| dir[Directory snapshot]
  snap -->|no| detach[detach / terminate]
  fs --> restore[Restore later]
  mem --> restore
  dir --> restore

members.note: Sandbox memory and filesystem snapshots checkpoint state and can be restored as exact clones.

evidence.title: Modal sandbox-snapshots - purpose
evidence.quote: Snapshots allow you to save your Sandbox's state and restore it later

evidence.title: Modal sandbox-snapshots - memory snapshot scope
evidence.quote: Sandbox memory snapshots are copies of a Sandbox's entire state, both in memory and on the filesystem

members.note: Sandboxes are advertised explicitly as the primitive for executing LLM-generated code.

evidence.title: Modal Sandbox - LLM code
evidence.quote: Execute code generated by a language model

evidence.title: Modal homepage - sandbox positioning
evidence.quote: Spin up thousands of isolated and secure Sandboxes to execute AI generated code

members.note: Sandbox is the headline isolation primitive: ephemeral, secure containers for untrusted code.

evidence.title: Modal Sandbox - secure containers
evidence.quote: secure containers for executing untrusted user or agent code on Modal

evidence.title: Modal Sandbox - isolated env
evidence.quote: Create isolated environments for running untrusted code

members.note: `@app.function(schedule=modal.Period(...))` or `modal.Cron(...)` runs a function on a cadence.

evidence.title: Modal cron - decorator schedule
evidence.quote: To schedule this function to run once per day, create a Modal App and attach our function to it with the `@app.function` decorator and a schedule parameter

evidence.title: Modal cron - Cron syntax
evidence.quote: `modal.Cron` gives you finer control using cron syntax

members.note: Modal's job-queue pattern is exactly start-tool-plus-poll-tool: .spawn() returns a FunctionCall handle immediately without waiting, the work runs in the background, and the result is fetched later via FunctionCall.get() or rehydrated from a stored id via FunctionCall.from_id().

evidence.title: modal
evidence.quote: A FunctionCall is a reference to an executed function call. Constructed using .spawn(...) on a Modal function ... acts as a reference to an ongoing function call that can be passed around and used to poll or fetch function results at some later time. get() waits indefinitely by default. It takes an optional timeout argument ... which can be set to 0 to poll for an output immediately.

alternatives.note: Modal provides sandboxed compute; Temporal provides durable workflow execution. Common pairing: Temporal workflow invokes Modal Sandboxes.
Different scopes: Restack is the agent runtime, Modal is the compute substrate.

references.title: Modal documentation
references.quote: Modal is an AI infrastructure platform that lets you: Run low latency inference with sub-second cold starts

references.title: modal-labs/modal-client
references.quote: convenient, on-demand access to serverless cloud compute from Python scripts on your local computer.

compositions.name: Restack
compositions.intent: Backend platform for building long-running AI agents on top of Temporal and Kubernetes, with workflows-as-code, MCP-exposed tools, ClickHouse-backed context, and a product-team-facing visual interface.
compositions.description: Restack is a vendor-managed agent platform for enterprise teams. Engineers write workflows in Python or TypeScript (`@restackio/ai`) by composing functions called via a `step` primitive; the platform runs these on a Temporal-backed durable engine deployed on Kubernetes so agents can run for weeks or months. Python functions are exposed to the agent as MCP tools, long-term context lives in a ClickHouse store, and product teams manage prompts, versions, and tests through a UI.
primary_use_cases: long-running enterprise AI agents (weeks-to-months horizons)
durable workflows composed from Python or TypeScript functions
MCP-tool integrations exposing in-house Python code
self-hosted agent deployments on AWS / GCP / Azure / on-prem
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Restack agents and workflows are durable Temporal-backed processes. A workflow is a multi-step function that uses the `step` primitive to call user-defined functions (the activities); the framework records each step so that on crash or restart the run resumes from the last committed step. Agents are long-running variants that can wait on events, call MCP tools, pull context from the ClickHouse store, and be scheduled via the platform's Schedules feature. Restack manages the underlying Kubernetes + Temporal stack so users do not run Temporal directly.

key_concepts.name: Workflow
key_concepts.summary: Multi-step orchestration coordinating model calls, API requests, and logic with durable state.

key_concepts.name: Step / Function
key_concepts.summary: `step()` primitive inside a workflow calls user-defined functions (the activities of the run).

key_concepts.name: Agent
key_concepts.summary: Long-running variant of a workflow that handles errors, retries, and interruptions automatically.

key_concepts.name: Context store
key_concepts.summary: Built-in long-term memory powered by ClickHouse; stores past interactions and retrievable context.

key_concepts.name: MCP-out-of-the-box
key_concepts.summary: Python functions are exposed to the agent as MCP tools by default.

key_concepts.name: Schedules
key_concepts.summary: Cron / calendar schedules on workflows, agents, and functions.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  ui[Product UI: prompts, versions, tests] --> agent[Agent / Workflow]
  agent --> step[step primitive]
  step --> fn[User Python or TS function]
  fn -.exposed as.-> mcp[MCP tool]
  agent --> ctx[Context store: ClickHouse]
  agent --> sched[Schedules: cron / calendar]
  agent -.runs on.-> dur[Durable Execution]
  dur -.powered by.-> temporal[Temporal + Kubernetes]
  fail[Crash / restart] -.replay.-> agent

members.note: Durable Execution lets Restack agents run for months, handling errors, retries, and interruptions automatically.

evidence.title: Restack architecture - durable execution
evidence.quote: Durable Execution: Agents can run for months, handling errors, retries, and interruptions automatically.

evidence.title: Restack workflows - long horizon
evidence.quote: Build AI applications with a system capable of coordinating steps like calling models, making API requests, and executing logic, all while maintaining state over weeks or even months reliably.

evidence.title: Restack workflows - schedule from agents
evidence.quote: Agents can call workflows.

members.note: Restack has a dedicated Schedules feature for workflows, agents, and functions; workflows can also be scheduled programmatically via `scheduleWorkflow`.

evidence.title: Restack workflows - scheduleWorkflow
evidence.quote: Any client can schedule the workflow to run.

evidence.title: Restack llms.txt - schedules feature
evidence.quote: Schedule workflows, agents, and functions with calendars and cron jobs

members.note: Workflows compose via the `step` primitive but no explicit step-budget cap is documented in the pages fetched; honest downgrade from first-class.

Workflows orchestrate functions and can call sub-workflows from an agent. No explicit router/supervisor primitive equivalent to AgentKit's was found on fetched pages; honest downgrade from first-class.

evidence.quote: The workflow resides in a separate file using the step primitive to orchestrate the functions.

members.note: MCP is the default tool surface; Python functions are exposed as tools the agent can call.

evidence.title: Restack architecture - MCP
evidence.quote: MCP Out of the Box: Restack uses the Model Context Protocol to expose Python functions as tools the agent can use.

members.note: MCP is the integration mechanism: Restack uses MCP to expose Python functions as agent tools.

evidence.title: Restack architecture - integrations
evidence.quote: Standard Python with MCP. Engineers build any integration using standard Python.

evidence.title: Restack architecture - MCP exposure
evidence.quote: Restack uses the Model Context Protocol to expose Python functions as tools the agent can use.

members.note: Built on Temporal and Kubernetes; Durable Execution is the marquee infrastructure property.

evidence.title: Restack architecture - infrastructure
evidence.quote: Powered by Kubernetes and Temporal. Built for agents that run for weeks or months.

evidence.title: Restack architecture - no DevOps
evidence.quote: Restack manages all infrastructure and upgrades. No DevOps team required.

members.note: An agent can hand slow work to a child workflow via childStart, which returns a durable workflow handle at once instead of blocking; the agent continues and collects the outcome through the handle later.

evidence.title: restack
evidence.quote: The child workflow executes independently of the agent. This method doesn't wait for the child workflow to finish before returning a promise; instead, it returns the child workflow handle.

alternatives.note: Restack runs on Temporal under the hood and adds an agent-shaped product layer, ClickHouse context, and a UI.
Peer durable-agent framework; AgentKit uses Inngest, Restack uses Temporal.
Different scopes: Restack is the agent runtime, Modal is the compute substrate.

compositions.name: Temporal
compositions.language: Go, Java, Python, TypeScript, .NET, PHP, Ruby
compositions.intent: Open-source durable-execution platform whose Workflows survive crashes, restarts, and infrastructure outages by replaying an event-sourced history; increasingly adopted as the substrate for long-running AI agents.
compositions.description: Temporal is a durable-execution platform originating as a fork of Uber's Cadence and built by Temporal Technologies. The server (Go) coordinates workflows and activities; SDKs in Go, Java, Python, TypeScript, .NET, PHP, and Ruby let developers write Workflow Definitions (deterministic functions) that invoke Activities (idempotent side-effecting functions). The Temporal Service persists every command and event in a per-workflow Event History; when a Worker crashes or restarts, Temporal replays the history to reconstruct in-memory state and continues from the last unfinished step. Signals, Queries, Updates, Schedules, Retry Policies, and the Saga compensation pattern are first-class primitives. Agent frameworks adopt Temporal to make agent loops crash-proof.
primary_use_cases: durable workflows for long-running, fault-tolerant business processes
AI-agent backbones with crash-proof tool loops
saga / compensation across distributed services
scheduled and signal-driven recurring jobs
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Workflow-as-code with deterministic replay. The agent loop is written as a Workflow Definition; each model call, tool call, or side effect runs as an Activity with built-in retries and timeouts. The Workflow's Event History is the source of truth: on Worker restart Temporal replays history step by step to rebuild the in-memory state, then resumes execution. Signals drive asynchronous inputs (user messages, approvals); Updates handle synchronous request/response; Queries inspect state without changing it; Schedules trigger periodic runs; Saga lists of compensating Activities roll back on failure.

key_concepts.name: Workflow
key_concepts.summary: Deterministic code defining a sequence of steps; the Event History is replayed to reconstruct state.

key_concepts.name: Activity
key_concepts.summary: Normal function executing a single well-defined action; auto-retried, can be non-deterministic, should be idempotent.

key_concepts.name: Signal / Query / Update
key_concepts.summary: Asynchronous writes, read-only inspections, and synchronous tracked writes against a running Workflow.

key_concepts.name: Retry Policy
key_concepts.summary: Declarative retry settings on Activities and Workflows; Activities retry by default.

key_concepts.name: Saga / Compensation
key_concepts.summary: Maintain a list of compensating Activities, executed in reverse on failure.

key_concepts.name: Schedule
key_concepts.summary: Periodic / cron triggers for Workflow Executions.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD
  user[Agent / business code] --> wf[Workflow Definition]
  wf --> act[Activity calls]
  act --> ext[LLM / tool / API]
  act --> retry{Activity failure?}
  retry -->|yes| policy[Retry Policy]
  policy --> act
  retry -->|no| hist[Event History append]
  wf --> sig[Signal / Update inbox]
  sig --> hist
  wf --> q[Query read-only]
  hist --> persist[Temporal Service persists]
  crash[Worker crash] -.replay.-> wf
  saga[Saga compensations] -.on failure.-> reverse[Execute in reverse order]
  sched[Schedule] -.triggers.-> wf

members.note: Workflows pick up exactly where they left off; the engine replays Event History to reconstruct state.

evidence.title: Temporal home - durable execution
evidence.quote: Temporal Workflows automatically capture state at every step, and in the event of failure, can pick up exactly where they left off.

evidence.title: Temporal workflows - replay
evidence.quote: Temporal doesn't restore memory from a snapshot. It starts the Workflow code from the beginning, replays the Event History step by step.

members.note: Saga pattern documented as a list of compensating Activities executed in reverse order on failure.

evidence.title: Temporal saga - definition
evidence.quote: The Saga pattern coordinates a sequence of operations where each operation has a compensating action to undo its effects.

evidence.title: Temporal saga - reverse order
evidence.quote: Execute compensating actions in reverse order to roll back previous operations.

members.note: Signals (async writes), Updates (sync tracked writes), and Queries (reads) drive running Workflows.

evidence.title: Temporal message-passing - Signals
evidence.quote: Signals are asynchronous write requests. They cause changes in the running Workflow, but you cannot await any response or error.

evidence.title: Temporal message-passing - Updates
evidence.quote: Updates are synchronous, tracked write requests. The sender of the Update can wait for a response on completion or an error on failure.

members.note: Activities retry by default per a declarative Retry Policy; failures fall through retry intervals before surfacing.

evidence.title: Temporal retry-policies - definition
evidence.quote: A Retry Policy is a collection of settings that tells Temporal how and when to try again after something fails in a Workflow Execution or Activity Task Execution.

evidence.title: Temporal retry-policies - default activity retries
evidence.quote: Temporal's default behavior is to automatically retry an Activity that fails, so transient or intermittent failures require no action on your part.

members.note: Temporal provides the substrate for replanning: ApplicationError with non_retryable=True bypasses retries and surfaces failure to the Workflow, which can then catch ActivityError and choose a new branch. Temporal does not prescribe a replanning algorithm — it provides the catch-and-branch substrate, so role stays at 'supported'.

evidence.title: Temporal retry-policies - non-retryable
evidence.quote: Non-Retryable Errors specify errors that shouldn't be retried.

evidence.title: Temporal Python - ApplicationError non_retryable
evidence.quote: An `ApplicationError` with `non_retryable=True` will never retry, regardless of the Retry Policy.

evidence.title: Temporal Python - catching activity failures
evidence.quote: Use Python's `try/except` blocks to handle Activity failures in your Workflow

members.note: Temporal Schedules are a first-class primitive for periodic / cron-driven Workflow Executions.

evidence.title: Temporal docs - SDK list
evidence.quote: Despite supporting multiple languages, and supporting many features, Temporal SDKs aim to make developers feel at home in their language.

evidence.title: Temporal home - state and timers
evidence.quote: The Temporal Service persists the state of your application and has built-in retries, task queues, signals, and timers

members.note: Each Workflow Execution has exclusive access to its local state; executions are uniquely identified and run concurrently and independently, communicating only via Signals and Activities.

evidence.title: Temporal workflows - history per execution
evidence.quote: Each Workflow Execution emits a series of Commands and processes a sequence of Events, which are recorded in an Event History.

evidence.title: Temporal workflow-execution - exclusive local state
evidence.quote: Each Temporal Workflow Execution has exclusive access to its local state.

evidence.title: Temporal workflow-execution - concurrent and independent
evidence.quote: It executes concurrently to all other Workflow Executions, and communicates with other Workflow Executions through Signals and the environment through Activities.

members.note: Activity timeouts (Schedule-To-Close, Start-To-Close, Schedule-To-Start, Heartbeat) and retry-policy max attempts function as per-step budgets; the engine does not surface a global step-count budget across the whole Workflow.

evidence.title: Temporal home - timeouts and retries
evidence.quote: Activities have built-in support for timeouts and retries!

evidence.title: Temporal - Schedule-To-Close Timeout
evidence.quote: The maximum amount of time allowed for the overall Activity Execution, from when the first Activity Task is scheduled to when the last Activity Task reaches a Closed status.

evidence.title: Temporal - Start-To-Close Timeout
evidence.quote: The maximum time allowed for a single Activity Task Execution.

members.note: Activities are the tool-use primitive: idempotent functions that call out to models, APIs, databases, MCP servers, etc.

evidence.title: Temporal activities - definition
evidence.quote: An Activity is a normal function or method that executes a single, well-defined action (either short or long running)

evidence.title: Temporal home - activities as interactions
evidence.quote: Temporal treats these interactions as Activities: functions that retry automatically and recover seamlessly.

members.note: Event-sourced Workflow with deterministic replay is the headline feature.

evidence.title: Temporal workflows - deterministic
evidence.quote: It has to make the same decisions when given the same history, which makes a Workflow deterministic.

evidence.title: Temporal workflows - history as source of truth
evidence.quote: This history is the source of truth for everything that happens in the Workflow.

members.note: Non-deterministic work runs in Activities whose results are recorded in event history and replayed on recovery, so workflow replay reuses them instead of re-executing.

evidence.title: Understanding Temporal - Temporal Platform Documentation
evidence.quote: The Temporal Service maintains a meticulous record of every step in your Workflows. By keeping a history of every step in your Workflow, it ensures that even if something goes wrong your Workflow can continue from the last successful point.

members.note: Temporal runs each tool call as a durable Activity that runs off the request path and is checkpointed; a workflow handle survives crashes and is resumed exactly where it left off, which is the durable handle plus result store the pattern describes.

evidence.title: temporal
evidence.quote: Your Activities are where the actual work happens: calling LLMs, invoking tools, making API requests. ... if the Workflow has to recover from a crash, it 'replays' your agent's progress to date, preserving previous tool call results via Event History.

evidence.title: Activity Execution - Temporal Platform Documentation
evidence.quote: Temporal guarantees that an Activity Task either runs or timeouts.

evidence.title: Understanding Temporal - Worker crash replay
evidence.quote: If the Worker crashes, the Worker uses the Event History to replay the code and recreate the state of the Workflow Execution to what it was immediately before the crash.

members.note: A running Workflow Execution can be halted out of band via the CLI, UI, or API by cancelling it (graceful) or terminating it (forceful), with no code change or redeploy.

evidence.title: Interrupt a Workflow - Cancellation and Termination
evidence.quote: Discover how Temporal enables you to gracefully handle Workflow interruptions through cancellations and terminations.

evidence.title: Interrupt a Workflow - Cancellation and Termination
evidence.quote: Understand how to stop a Workflow cleanly with cancellation, allowing for proper cleanup and state management. For situations where a Workflow is stuck, termination provides an immediate solution, ensuring your applications remain robust and responsive.

members.note: Decouples a durable event-sourced workflow (immutable Event History) from stateless workers that execute one step and report back; the workflow is reconstructed by replaying the log, enabling pause/resume/replay.

evidence.title: Durable Execution meets AI: Why Temporal is ideal for AI agents
evidence.quote: Temporal activities facilitate durability through event sourcing, persisting each workflow step as an immutable event so that completed actions aren't re-executed. ... Agent Workers are a fleet of stateless processes whose only job is to ask the Temporal Cluster for work, execute a single step ... and report the result back.

members.note: Temporal ships the saga pattern: register compensations per step and run them (including in parallel) when a later step fails.

evidence.quote: saga.addCompensation(activities::cancelHotel, info.getClientId()); activities.bookHotel(info);

alternatives.note: Restack is a managed agent platform built on top of Temporal.
AgentKit on Inngest is a TS-only peer durable-execution stack.
Modal supplies sandboxed compute; Temporal supplies the durable control plane.

references.title: Temporal homepage
references.quote: Build applications that never lose state, even when everything else fails.

references.title: temporalio/temporal
references.quote: Temporal is a durable execution platform that enables developers to build scalable applications without sacrificing productivity or reliability.

references.title: Temporal home - durable AI agents
references.quote: Develop agents that survive real-world chaos, reliable MCP & orchestrate training pipelines.

anti_patterns_avoided.note: Per-activity automatic retries plus durable history isolate a failed step and resume from the last successful point instead of cascading the failure.

evidence.title: Temporal — understanding Temporal
evidence.quote: If an Activity fails, Temporal automatically retries it based on your configuration. ... By keeping a history of every step in your Workflow, it ensures that even if something goes wrong your Workflow can continue from the last successful point.

anti_patterns_avoided.note: Start-To-Close timeouts and activity heartbeats detect a worker that has silently stopped making progress, so a delegated task cannot vanish unnoticed.

evidence.title: Temporal — detecting activity failures
evidence.quote: An Activity Heartbeat is a ping from the Worker that is executing the Activity to the Temporal Service. Each ping informs the Temporal Service that the Activity Execution is making progress and the Worker has not crashed.

anti_patterns_avoided.note: Durable execution uses idempotency keys so a retried external operation returns the original result instead of performing it twice.

evidence.title: Temporal — idempotency and durable execution
evidence.quote: Most external services support idempotency keys -- unique identifiers that prevent duplicate operations. When the service receives a request with a key it has already processed, it returns the original result instead of performing the operation again.

anti_patterns_avoided.note: Workflow code must be deterministic on replay; non-deterministic LLM/API calls go in Activities that execute outside the replay path, so a replay does not diverge from the original run.

evidence.title: Temporal — workflow definition
evidence.quote: Workflow code must be deterministic to support replay. ... To handle non-deterministic operations like API calls, LLM/AI invocations, database queries, and other external interactions, put them in Activities. Activities execute outside the replay path and are automatically retried so they do not cause non-determinism errors.

compositions.name: Pathway
compositions.intent: Pathway is a Python and Rust framework that builds streaming data and RAG pipelines on a differential-dataflow engine, keeping vector indexes synchronised with their sources as data changes.
compositions.description: Pathway runs the same pipeline code over batch and streaming data, with a Rust engine that recomputes results incrementally as inputs change. Its LLM extension provides parsers, embedders, splitters, and an in-memory real-time vector index, plus document-store components that re-index automatically on new data. Teams use it to build retrieval-augmented generation pipelines whose indexes stay current without a separate batch re-indexing job. The framework integrates with LlamaIndex and LangChain.
primary_use_cases: real-time retrieval-augmented generation pipelines
live document indexing and vector search
incremental ETL over streaming and batch data
continuously synchronised knowledge bases for agents
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Pathway is a data-pipeline runtime rather than an agent loop. Connectors ingest documents and change events; the differential-dataflow engine parses, chunks, embeds, and indexes them incrementally; and a vector or document store serves retrieval queries. A question-answering layer retrieves the top documents for a query and passes them to an LLM, with the index kept in sync as sources change.

members.note: A Python/Rust differential-dataflow framework that processes documents into RAG features incrementally as a continuous stream and keeps a live vector index synchronised with sources in real time, no batch re-indexing.

evidence.title: Why Pathway
evidence.quote: real-time data indexes allowing you to effortlessly synchronize your index with data sources in real time

evidence.title: pathwaycom/pathway README
evidence.quote: Pathway Live Data Framework provides an LLM extension with all the utilities to integrate LLMs with your data pipelines (LLM wrappers, parsers, embedders, splitters), including an in-memory real-time Vector Index, and integrations with LLamaIndex and LangChain.

members.note: The LLM extension ships question-answering components that retrieve the k best documents and pass them to an LLM, plus an AdaptiveRAGQuestionAnswerer that adjusts retrieval to optimise token usage, on top of a document store that re-indexes itself as new data arrives.

evidence.title: LLM xpack overview
evidence.quote: Retrieval Augmented Generation

members.note: Pathway's BaseRAGQuestionAnswerer is a standard retrieve-then-generate RAG: given a query it pulls the k best documents from the vector store and sends them with the question to the LLM, with knowledge living in the external index rather than the model's parameters.

evidence.title: LLM xpack overview | Pathway
evidence.quote: BaseRAGQuestionAnswerer is a standard RAG, that given a query obtains `k` best documents from the vector store, and sends them along the question to the LLM chat.

key_concepts.name: DocumentStore
key_concepts.summary: A pipeline component that automatically indexes documents and updates itself when new data arrives, so the served index reflects the source without a separate batch re-indexing job.

key_concepts.name: Differential dataflow engine
key_concepts.summary: A scalable Rust engine that performs incremental computation, recomputing only what changed as inputs update, and runs the same Python pipeline code over both batch and streaming data.

key_concepts.name: In-memory real-time Vector Index
key_concepts.summary: A vector index kept inside the running pipeline that stays synchronised with data sources in real time, serving similarity queries for RAG over live documents.

key_concepts.name: AdaptiveRAGQuestionAnswerer
key_concepts.summary: A RAG question-answerer that limits the number of documents sent to the LLM chat to save tokens, adjusting retrieval to the query.

references.title: pathwaycom/pathway README
references.quote: Pathway Live Data Framework provides an LLM extension with all the utilities to integrate LLMs with your data pipelines (LLM wrappers, parsers, embedders, splitters), including an in-memory real-time Vector Index, and integrations with LLamaIndex and LangChain.

references.title: Why Pathway
references.quote: Pathway Live Data Framework offers real-time data indexes (vector search, full text search, and more) allowing you to effortlessly synchronize your index with data sources in real time.

references.title: LLM xpack overview | Pathway
references.quote: it is easy to create in Pathway a pipeline serving as a `DocumentStore`, which automatically indexes documents and gets updated upon new data.

alternatives.note: Streamkap also keeps a vector store in sync with source data in real time, but as a managed CDC streaming service rather than a Python/Rust dataflow framework you write pipelines in.
Pathway integrates with LlamaIndex; LlamaIndex provides the RAG orchestration and query layer while Pathway supplies the live, incrementally-updated index underneath.

compositions.pattern_composition:
flowchart TD fw["Pathway"] fw --> p1["Streaming Feature Pipeline
(core)"] fw --> p2["Agentic RAG
(supported)"] fw --> p3["Naive RAG
(supported)"]

compositions.name: Streamkap
compositions.intent: Streamkap is a managed platform that streams change-data-capture events from databases into destinations such as vector stores in real time so downstream embeddings stay in sync with the source.
compositions.description: Streamkap connects databases, applications, and warehouses through change-data-capture and event streaming, moving data from source to destination with sub-50ms latency. For AI workloads it streams each insert, update, and delete from the source into a vector database, triggering an embedding update so retrieval data does not go stale. It processes only the rows that changed rather than re-embedding the whole dataset in batch.
primary_use_cases: real-time CDC pipelines from databases to vector stores
keeping embeddings and RAG context current with source data
streaming ETL into warehouses and applications
incremental embedding updates for recommendation and agent context
compositions.agent_loop_shape: Streamkap is a streaming-pipeline service, not an agent loop. It captures change events from a source database, optionally transforms them in flight, and delivers them to a sink. When the sink is a vector store, each captured change drives an embedding update, so the index served to a downstream agent or RAG system reflects the source as it changes.

members.note: Uses change-data-capture so the source database stays the single writer; every insert/update/delete is streamed in real time and triggers an embedding update that keeps the vector store in sync.

evidence.title: Streaming CDC Events to Vector Databases for Real-Time AI - Streamkap
evidence.quote: use CDC to stream database changes to your vector database in real time. Every insert, update, and delete in the source triggers an embedding update

evidence.title: Streaming CDC Events to Vector Databases for Real-Time AI - Streamkap
evidence.quote: But there is a gap most teams overlook: the data in the vector database gets stale the moment the source database changes.

members.note: Rather than re-embedding the full dataset on a batch schedule, Streamkap streams each source change as it happens and processes only what actually changed, building the embedding feature incrementally as a continuous stream.

evidence.title: Streaming CDC Events to Vector Databases for Real-Time AI - Streamkap
evidence.quote: CDC eliminates all of these by streaming each change as it happens, so you only process what actually changed.

members.note: The vector-sync path is a staged stream pipeline - source DB to a CDC engine to Kafka to an embedding service to the vector DB - where each stage consumes the previous stage's events and emits to the next, the classic chain of filters connected by a queue.

evidence.title: Streaming CDC Events to Vector Databases for Real-Time AI - Streamkap
evidence.quote: Source DB ──> CDC Engine ──> Kafka ──> Embedding Service ──> Vector DB

evidence.title: Streaming CDC Events to Vector Databases for Real-Time AI - Streamkap
evidence.quote: Kafka: Buffers and orders change events

key_concepts.name: CDC engine
key_concepts.summary: Captures every INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE from the source database's transaction log so downstream consumers see each change as an event rather than re-scanning the table.

key_concepts.name: Embedding service
key_concepts.summary: A pipeline stage that consumes CDC events and decides whether to generate a new embedding, so embedding cost is proportional to the rate of change rather than dataset size.

key_concepts.name: Sources and destinations
key_concepts.summary: Streamkap connects a variety of sources to destinations and streams data sub-second between them, with pipelines configured rather than coded.

references.title: Streaming CDC Events to Vector Databases for Real-Time AI - Streamkap
references.quote: The solution is straightforward: use CDC to stream database changes to your vector database in real time.

references.title: Streamkap Documentation
references.quote: Serverless, real-time data integration platform for streaming data sub-second from sources to destinations

references.title: Streaming CDC Events to Vector Databases for Real-Time AI - Streamkap
references.quote: For AI applications like RAG, agent context, and recommendation engines, this freshness is the difference between useful and unreliable.

alternatives.note: Pathway also keeps vector indexes synchronised with sources in real time, but as a self-hosted Python/Rust dataflow framework rather than a managed serverless CDC service.
Temporal is a durable workflow engine for orchestrating agent logic; it pairs with rather than replaces Streamkap's job of keeping the retrieval index fresh underneath such agents.
